replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: Heh. Guns of Navarone is an utter CLASSIC. And packed with so many stars who were really worthy of the name STAR! David Niven is superb. Each time I watch it, he reminds me of me. I am exactly like his character, dont trust authority, constantly questioning bad decisions and wondering if its all worth it and if I am doing the right thing. He is so well written and at logger heads with Pecks character from the first scene. Yet he does his job and knows what he is doing. The climax of all the sniping with his deduction of the who is the traitor and the fact he cant bring himself to shoot her is brilliant. I could not have done it either. Then there is the rest of the cast. Anthony Quinn, Anthony Quail, Gregory Peck, Stanley Baker..on and on and on. Even down to them casting Richard Harris in nothing more than a cameo and he is brilliant in it too! And its a war film that is not a war film, and its not historically accurate but is accurate in its portrayal of the German treatment of Greece and its people. They dont make films like this anymore and if they tried they would not be able to get a cast to carry it off like this. I have my own projector and my entire living room wall is the screen, with the full surround set up. I spent over a year building it all up and it was to watch film like Guns of Navarone and Blade Runner and Alien etc as they were originally shown, but in my own home. Last weekend I was watching Battle of the Bulge. This weekend I cant decided if its going to be Roller Ball or Bridge at Remagen....I love my cinema....

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: @JasonOfStrat Scary. I saw it with my mate at the cinema when it originally came out. I was a teen still at school. And to us it was a good film, fun and enjoyable. But it was not a standout at the time as there were so many good films to choose from. We were going to see stuff like Wrath of Khan, Poltergeist, E.T. Rambo, Ghostbusters was out just the year before, The Thing, Return of the Jedi. Temple of Doom, The Goonies, Terminator, Predator, Robocop, Platoon, Voyage Home, Flight of the Navigator the list is endless and so Back to the Future was another great and fun film at the time, but it was not the standout classic as there was so many others. To us it was normal for films to be this good, we took it for granted, enjoyed it, laughed and talked about it and waited for the next great release in a month or so and looked forward to seeing BTTF on video. We were spoiled, lucky and took it all for granted a lot of the time. And here I am forty years later and it did become a beloved classic, it always will be and its now thrown into sharp relief by the fact that modern films are so incredibly bad, soulless, bland and no longer do much new. Forty years of progress and cinema has declined and Hollywood is now failing. Stars are no longer the draw and most people check the films premise so carefully to avoid woke / girl boss and dross. Instead of just turning up to be entertained like we did, as we knew it would be fun.

 Eighty years ago, in Munich, Adolf Hitler staged two exhibitions which defined the way we think about modern art. The Great German Art show displayed the artists he approved of, the Degenerate Art show displayed the artists he despised. replies.author: @katiemiles637
replies.text: @jacobe3579inflation and stagnant wages has killed the middle class.  Covid lockdowns destroyed most small businesses.  People who started planning a family have had their plans destroyed after they had children.  Congrats for everything going to plan in your life, but you are an arrogant ass if you don’t understand that for every one person it works out for there are 90 that it did not.  The top 10% in America make $99k or more.  Average Americans are at the 50% mark at around $66k.  Many people work hard, don’t take government money, and are barely making it.  After taxes, house payment/rent, utilities, groceries, various insurances, phones, gas, etc.  the average american has a couple hundred of dollars a month in disposable income.  Taking a family of four to a movie is $80 in tickets, 1 combo and 2 kids combo is roughly $30-$40.  So roughly half of disposable income for the month is given up for one movie that is free in two weeks - a month on streaming.  Congratulations on living in the top 10%, but the fact you talk down to the other %90 makes me double down on my initial comment.

Barbie							(2023)	\$636.2 million

replies.text:
For me the issue of cinemas has been that unless you pay insane money the seats are just too uncomfortable. I'm over 6' and the last movie I paid to see was Serenity back in 2003. That was also on a rainy Tuesday afternoon with only about 8 other people in the same room with discounted prices.

Since then I have developed serious back/spine problems that makes sitting on public transport, waiting room chairs and even sitting at a desk pretty much non-starters. It's the reason I stopped work 20+ years ago and an now retired early at 62.

I didn't replace my old Toshiba 36" CRT (purchased around 2000) until 2016 as up until then all the flatscreens I had seen were significantly inferio, with poor contrast, colour and sound. It was not until LG decided to stop making standard HD screens and moved over to only producing 4K screen, did the price crash that I got one of theirs. At that point every other TV maker was still selling 4K TVs at well over £1000, but suddenly LG started selling them at 1080/HD prices so my 49" cost £600.

My current TV (now 2 years old and the 2022 model that was on sale at less than half price) is a 65" LG mini-led backlit marvel, it's main duty and reason for upgrading is that it's my main gaming PC's monitor, as it has VRR from 48-120Hz. As such it gets vast amounts of games and YouTube content, my sister allows me to use one of her two devices for Netflix and everything else is the DVDs and Blue-rays I buy (then rip and recode to save disk space).

Why would I want to spend more money than the cost of a Blue-ray for one ticket, just to sit in an uncomfortable cinema with loads of people most of whom are not even watching as they chat, text and otherwise eff-about.

replies.author: @garanceadrosehn9691
replies.text: @jakeviolet2195 — I remember some successful author saying that the people now writing detective/mystery novels today don't know how to write a detective story, because they grew up watching TV/Movies instead of reading books.  One of his points was that if you're reading a book, then the only thing you *have* is the story.  The author has to create scenes in the minds of their readers, and they have to have a story (and story arc) that's captivating enough that the reader keeps wanting to read to the next page.

So many AI visuals are potentially cool but still most of it is not good enough to suspend belief to enjoy a modern movie.  If you can't make the AI scene look seriously real why ruin a movie with it?  Then there are just the lack of a grounded moral base in most modern movies or just the wrong moral choices of the plot so shock & deviance will only take a movie so far.  My guess is maybe profit and loss will correct the problems sometime in the future, but I dunno.

replies.author: @TimothyHTrain
replies.text: @mikecrooks8085 sequelitis is definitely part of the problem. Obviously it sucks a lot of the originality out of the plot (this is the problem that Star Wars had almost as soon as it was made). Then, it poses a problem to writers who come in later in the series - how do you make a plot using the *same* characters that is *just* as epic as the original film, just as mind-bending, when all those characters have already been through all that? And make it believable?

replies.author: @ARCNA442
replies.text: @TimothyHTrain This. If Hollywood just let the screenwriters go, I'm sure they could come up with tons of great scripts. The problem is that when you have hundreds of millions of dollars riding on a movie, it's the businessmen and lawyers and celebrity personalities who end up dictating the story, not the creatives. And being the shortsighted people they are, the businessmen's response to the problem is "well, let's just throw more money at it to make up for the lack of creativity" turning the whole thing into a self-reinforcing doom spiral.

replies.author: @Sultschiem
replies.text:
​@TheSLashera It is among the most influential of that era, with 4 main influencing titles:
My Hero Academia, One Punch man, Mob Psycho 100, Daemon Slayer.

Those redefined shonen anime series with their pacing, fights & animation. Were previous shone Animes wasted a ton of time, especially with fillers, those 4 did not and they changed the landscape quite a bit, forcing others to step it up. Especially given One Piece's Whole Cake Arc, the vast difference became painfully apparent... entire episodes had less than 5 minutes of new content.

Daemon Slayer was the first to outright adapt a Manga Arc into a movie outright for its main running story.

And before someone mentions Dragon Ball: The movie "Battle of the Gods" came first, before a Manga chapter and a new anime series released 2 years afterwards due to its success.

author: @TheHilltopPillbox
text:
Had an interesting discussion with a colleague, and although he is 15 years younger than I am, we both agreed that there really is no more shared cultural experience when it comes to movies and TV shows. 

The last one that had any sort of wide impact would be Game of Thrones, but that was almost 8 years ago.

We still talk about movies from the 80's, and we even reference them in daily life. The original Star Wars trilogy, Back to the Future, Indiana Jones, Ferris Beuller's Day Off, etc., are all recognizable to people of a certain age because they were quality films that stuck with us. No politics. No "message". Just fun, and we flocked to the cinemas to see them, and in many cases, more than once.

Then we bought the VHS tapes and proudly displayed them on our shelves.

I agree that it is much easier to sit in the comfort of my home, but I do miss the days of the shared cultural experience that came with cheering for the hero, boo-ing the villain, and laughing at the antics we watched as a roomful of strangers.

And it ain't ever coming back.

I think the reason it's not coming back, is that now with the streaming platforms industry, there is just so many movies and series coming out every month, none can become the shared cultural experiment : there simply is too many. 
Still in the 2000's, most people, at least in developped countries, went to see the star wars prelogy, Iron man I, The Lord of the Rings, Inception, No country for old men, black hawk fall, kill bill, 300, gladiator, the sixth sense....
It was the movies you couldn't miss, and if you failed to catch them in theaters, you would buy the dvd later or see it on an illegal streaming site. 
USA would watch its own movies, other countries would watch their own movies and USA movies. 
Now platforms pull out so much stuff, from the whole world, accessible to the whole world : you have little chance to have your colleagues, and even long time friends, liking the same material as you.

replies.author: @seanmadson8524
replies.text:
It isn't that the left or the right are inherently bad at creative pursuits, as both sides paradoxically claim their opposition are bad at artistic expression. This is not the metric by which the ability to be amusingly creative is measured. Nothing can cleanly measure such a subjective concept, but there is one thing that consistently gets in the way of creativity.

Anyone who raises a message or ulterior motive above the goal of heartfelt creative expression is going to significantly increase the difficulty with and complexity of said expression. It is possible to make good art while focusing on a message, but that is the rare exception to the rule, not the norm. Just because there is good art with a message doesn't mean it is easy to make good art with a message, it is in fact drastically harder since the message has to resonate with the audience while simultaneously being amusing enough to be worth watching/reading/etc.

Case and point: the Satanic Panic was an attempt to restrict media available to everyone that didn't adhere to the beliefs of a very specific group which led to massive amounts of bland, pathetically sanitized works that even struggled to be amusing to the target audience. In more recent years, the politically correct/woke movement stemming primarily from the left side of the political spectrum did essentially the same thing, focusing on identity politics instead of religion, and led to lame media for basically the same set of reasons.
